reviewer: Codex independent track-07 lyric and prompt reviewer

evidence: Filtered-synth intro, six two-bar lines per Verse, four per initial Chorus and Bridge, six-line final Chorus, and lyric-free eight-bar Coda match the canonical TrackSpec.

The filtered pad, reverse guitar, warm bass, support-only pulse, bridge-only piano, suspended Bridge pulse, narrow dynamics, and varied final return all trace to the locked fingerprint and prompt fields.
The active payload retains no unconfirmed percussion-lead label: support-only pulse is positive direction and percussion-led is confined to the Exclusion Prompt.

The Basic Prompt contains positive arrangement, form, vocal, and mix direction; the Exclusion Prompt contains percussion-led, violin, cello, piano-intro, range, and belting prohibitions; the title remains outside all blocks.
Lyrics contains only supported section tags and complete lyric text, with no production or exclusion instructions.
Dream-pop lullaby styling, filtered synth, reverse guitar, warm bass, support-only pulse, dim top, quiet lows, narrow dynamics, and a long dark tail reinforce the quiet rainy ride.
Bridge piano enters only when the pulse suspends, providing contrast through harmony and texture rather than loudness.
No audible result is inferred; this score reflects only the consistency of the written form, arrangement, harmony, and mix instructions.
I'll Keep the Road Quiet is distinctive and directly names the driver's promise to protect the exhausted passenger's sleep.
The first two Choruses retain Sleep through the lights and the road-quiet promise, while the expanded final Chorus converts that promise into the completed past-tense result.
The second Chorus's nearly-home variation and the final Chorus's completed-arrival language implement the specified varied return without changing the hook's meaning.
The English is natural and performable, including You close the door and fasten your seat belt, I keep below the posted speed, and Then park the car before I wake you.
The Verse lines remain approximately eight to eleven syllables and each has two bars, leaving adequate room for natural lexical stress without dense compression.
The first three lines of the first two Choruses are identical; their fourth lines preserve the SLEEP-LIGHTS and HOME-centered stress frame while allowing a one-syllable varied ending.

The expanded final Chorus uses closely corresponding past-tense phrases such as I'll take/I took and needs saying/needed saying, while its additional two lines are supported by the larger twelve-bar allocation.
This is a text-level prosody judgment only and does not claim that a generated melody or pronunciation will follow the intended stress.
Both twelve-bar Verses have six complete lines, both initial eight-bar Choruses and the eight-bar Bridge have four complete lines, and the twelve-bar final Chorus has six complete lines.
The non-vocal Intro and Coda contain no lyric lines, and all repeated passages are written in full without shorthand.
The current output passes deterministic validation at 216.0 planned seconds with 30 lyric lines and 232 English units.

Positive instrumentation specifies filtered synth, reverse guitar, warm bass, support-only pulse, and bridge piano; unusable percussion-led, violin, cello, and piano-intro results are confined to Exclusion.
Bridge piano does not conflict with piano intro because the prompt explicitly delays piano until the Bridge.

The Vocal field uses positive outcomes such as clear English, stable tone, and clean joins, while active Basic, Exclusion, Title, and Lyrics fields contain none of the suppressed artifact-linked cue families.
Track 07 preserves the rainy-night, low-register, narrow-dynamic playlist identity while occupying its quietest interior point through dream-pop lullaby color and a sleeping passenger.

Its filtered-synth and reverse-guitar opening, support-only pulse, bridge-only piano, and expanded final Chorus distinguish it from the preceding radio, confession, separation, route-choice, family-rescue, and parked-apology narratives.
The narrative goal is caretaking through an even ride rather than romantic rumination or confrontation, giving the track a clear playlist role.

The passenger finishes a 1:15 late shift, reports a persistent headache, fastens the seat belt, and falls asleep, giving the driver a clear reason to dim the dash, choose the river road, and maintain an even pace.
The driver keeps below the posted speed, reports the remaining ten minutes when the passenger briefly wakes, and allows the passenger to settle again.
At the destination, the driver takes the building ramp, parks before waking the passenger, switches off the engine, and only then touches the passenger's shoulder.
A forty-eight-minute journey from 1:15 to the appearance of the home street at 2:03 is plausible for a rainy late-night river-road route.

The seat belt, clear lanes, reduced speed, signal stop, spoken ten-minute update, building ramp, parking, engine shutdown, and gentle wake-up all behave truthfully within the established caretaking premise.
The canonical premise establishes a late-shift departure but does not identify a hospital; the lyrics correctly preserve the confirmed post-shift context without inventing an unsupported workplace.
Time progresses from the 1:15 departure through the river road, bridge, depot, narrowing road, signal, final ten minutes, and 2:03 arrival without reversal or unexplained displacement.
The passenger falls asleep before the bridge, wakes once near a signal, receives the ten-minute update, settles again, and remains asleep until after parking.

The final physical sequence is coherent: the home street appears, the bakery is passed, the car takes the building ramp, parks, the engine is switched off, and only then is the passenger touched and awakened.
I remains the driver and caretaker throughout, while you remains the exhausted late-shift passenger.
Our street, our building, and home consistently describe the shared destination without introducing a third speaker or changing the addressee.
The imperative Sleep through the lights and the final statement You slept through the lights preserve the same speaker-to-passenger relationship.
I dim the dash and take the river road gives the passenger's headache and fatigue concrete consequences in the driver's choices.
You wake once when the signal changes is supported by the stopped or restarting car, and the following ten-minute update explains the brief exchange.
Then park the car before I wake you explicitly establishes the required safety and action order.
I switch the engine off beneath our building followed by I touch your shoulder and say, we're here preserves the parked-car sequence.
The road is still functions as a context-supported closing image: the journey has ended and the quiet promised by the hook remains after arrival.
